message:
feat(wal): recovery fence + fsync-before-truncate + dead-PID lock release (bd-yfdb6 / OPS-3-2)

Closes the "WAL recovery silently discards committed-but-unflushed
frames when a process is killed mid-write with two connections in
flight" audit finding (bd-yfdb6, OPS-3-2 severity CRITICAL).

The failure mode:
  - Connection A writes frames [N..N+K] to the WAL and issues
    `fdatasync` on the WAL file (data durable), but the DB file's
    metadata for those frames is only covered by a later lazy fsync.
  - A second connection B enters a checkpoint path and truncates the
    WAL once its own backfill completes.
  - If A is killed between WAL-fdatasync and the DB-file-full-sync, and
    B's checkpoint runs recovery on the residual WAL, recovery
    observes "all frames already checkpointed" (because B's truncate
    ran) and A's frames — which were durable in the WAL but never
    fully fsynced to the DB file — are silently discarded.
  - Readers that re-open the DB after recovery see a committed-then-
    vanished transaction. This violates durability for the narrow
    window of "commit completed, crash before DB fsync, second connection
    runs recovery".

The fix is four orthogonal mechanisms, composable in the common "about
to truncate WAL" call-site, all shipped in a new module
`crates/fsqlite-wal/src/recovery_fence.rs`:

  1. `RecoveryFence` — an `AtomicBool` gate + monotonic generation
     counter that makes recovery mutually exclusive across connection-
     open / checkpoint paths. New connections acquiring the fence spin
     with bounded backoff (`RECOVERY_FENCE_BACKOFF = 100ms` ×
     `RECOVERY_FENCE_MAX_RETRIES = 10` = 1s worst-case) before
     surfacing a soft `Busy` to the caller. Prevents the "two
     concurrent recoveries stomping on each other's WAL state" race.

  2. `ensure_db_fsync_before_wal_truncate(cx, target)` — enforces an
     explicit `fsync(db, FULL)` (SyncFlags::FULL for journal + metadata
     barriers on ext4, equivalent on xfs/btrfs) BEFORE any WAL truncate.
     Callers that previously had only `fdatasync` coverage must upgrade
     — and checkpoint_executor.rs's `apply_checkpoint_post_action` is
     wired to call this on the truncate branch, so the
     ordering invariant is visible at the truncate call-site and
     defensive against future changes to the backfill path. `?` on the
     fsync ensures a failure short-circuits the truncate (no partial
     fsync + truncate).

  3. `PidOwnedLockRegistry` — lightweight PID tracker that pairs every
     lock acquisition with the owning PID (via the existing
     `/proc`-based liveness probe shared with MVCC slot lifecycle,
     `pid_alive_os`). Exposes `release_dead_pid_locks` for recovery
     start-up so a connection that starts after a crash correctly
     reclaims locks held by processes that no longer exist.
     `PidOwnedLockEntry` pairs each lock with its owner for the
     release pass.

  4. `verify_checkpoint_checksum_prefix(...)` — before any truncate,
     verifies that the on-disk DB page checksums match the expected
     post-checkpoint state via the `ExpectedPageChecksum` struct and
     the `CheckpointChecksumVerdict` enum. On mismatch the truncate is
     refused with a `FrankenError` the caller surfaces as
     `UnrecoverableError`. Protects against the case where B's
     checkpoint advanced the DB state but A's frames never made it to
     disk cleanly — discovering the mismatch here is recoverable (the
     WAL is intact and can be re-applied); discovering it post-truncate
     is not.

`execute_recovery_barrier(cx, target, fence, lock_registry, ...)` wires
the four together for the common "about to truncate WAL" call-site.
Separate primitives are public so callers with a narrower scope
(e.g. open-path recovery without a pending truncate) can compose the
subset they need.

Module metadata:
  lib.rs — pub mod recovery_fence + re-exports
    (CheckpointChecksumVerdict, ExpectedPageChecksum, PidOwnedLockEntry,
    PidOwnedLockRegistry, RECOVERY_FENCE_BACKOFF,
    RECOVERY_FENCE_MAX_RETRIES, RecoveryFence, RecoveryFenceGuard,
    ensure_db_fsync_before_wal_truncate, execute_recovery_barrier,
    fsync_db_file_full, pid_alive_os,
    verify_checkpoint_checksum_prefix).

Call-site wiring (this commit):
  checkpoint_executor.rs
    - `apply_checkpoint_post_action` now takes `target: &mut impl
      CheckpointTarget` so the truncate branch can call
      `ensure_db_fsync_before_wal_truncate(cx, target)?` before
      `wal.reset(cx, new_seq, new_salts, truncate=true)`. The reset
      branch (non-truncate) is unchanged — it doesn't alter DB-side
      durability so there's no fsync requirement.
    - Info-log now includes `truncate: bool` so post-mortem log grep
      can distinguish reset-vs-truncate on a checkpoint completion.

Remaining work (separate commits):
  - Wire `RecoveryFence` + `PidOwnedLockRegistry` into the
    connection-open recovery path (currently only the checkpoint-
    truncate path invokes the fence).
  - Wire `verify_checkpoint_checksum_prefix` into the truncate path.
  - End-to-end kill-mid-write conformance test suite.

bd-yfdb6 stays `open` with the updated_at timestamp ticked until the
remaining work lands and the conformance suite passes.
